steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Both the clause before the blank ('The grammar of the Gujarati language includes several noun cases common to most Indo-Aryan languages') and the clause after ('the locative case, for instance, indicates that an action is occurring at, on, or near a particular noun') are independent clauses. Find the punctuation that joins them correctly.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — comma only: joining two independent clauses with only a comma is a comma splice — a grammar error. ✗
  • Choice B — comma only (variant): same comma-splice error as A. ✗
  • Choice C — semicolon: a semicolon correctly joins two independent clauses without a coordinating conjunction. ✓
  • Choice D — semicolon + 'such as': 'such as' introduces examples within a clause, not a new independent clause. Placing it here creates an awkward, ungrammatical construction. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
